title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Viability of Nuclear Power in a Green Future

texts.A: Nuclear energy remains the only scalable solution to our carbon crisis. The intermittent nature of wind and solar means they cannot provide the reliable 'baseload' power required for modern industry. While the fear of disaster is high, the statistical safety record of nuclear is superior to almost any other energy source. The real obstacle isn't the technology or the waste—it is the political cowardice that refuses to invest in the next generation of small modular reactors that could power our cities for centuries.
texts.B: To call nuclear power 'green' is to ignore the toxic legacy of uranium mining and the unresolved problem of radioactive waste. We are essentially leaving a ticking time bomb for future generations to manage. The massive capital costs and decades-long construction times of nuclear plants mean they are too slow to help us meet our 2030 climate goals. We should instead focus on massive investments in battery storage and smart grids, which can make renewables as reliable as any traditional power plant.
texts.C: The binary choice between 'nuclear' and 'renewables' is a false one. A diversified energy portfolio is the only way to ensure national security. I agree that the construction of large-scale traditional reactors is no longer economically sound. However, I share the view that the baseload problem of wind and solar is real. Nuclear should serve as a secondary, stabilizing force in the grid, but only if we can solve the waste issue through international cooperation rather than dumping it in deep geological repositories that may fail.
texts.D: The economic argument against nuclear power is now insurmountable. As the cost of solar and wind continues to plummet, nuclear becomes a financial black hole for taxpayers. I share the skepticism regarding the safety of nuclear waste, but my primary concern is the risk of proliferation—the more countries that have nuclear energy programs, the harder it becomes to prevent the spread of nuclear weapons. We must move toward a decentralized, 100% renewable energy system that is both safer and cheaper.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the reliability of a grid powered solely by renewables?
questions.explanation: Expert A believes renewables are too intermittent for industrial baseload. Expert B argues that battery storage and smart grids can make renewables just as reliable.

questions.question: Which expert shares D's concern about the financial costs associated with nuclear energy?
questions.explanation: Expert D calls nuclear a 'financial black hole.' Expert B also highlights the 'massive capital costs' and long construction times that make it impractical.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the necessity of a stable power supply (baseload)?
questions.explanation: Expert C agrees that the 'baseload problem' of renewables is real. Expert A makes this the central point of their argument for nuclear energy.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the ethical implications of using nuclear technology?
questions.explanation: Expert B focuses on the 'toxic legacy' and 'ticking time bomb' for future generations. Expert A, however, argues that nuclear is the only 'pragmatic response' to the climate crisis, implying a different moral priority.
